result.markdown:
--- title: "Data" subtitle: "Survey Design, Conflict Measurement, and Sample Description" date: last-modified --- ## Survey Design ### The Burundi Priority Household Survey (HPS) The primary data source is the **Burundi Priority Household Panel Survey** (Enquête Prioritaire des Ménages), a three-wave longitudinal household survey covering rural Burundi. ::: {.callout-note} **Full citation:** Burundi Priority Household Panel Survey 1998–2007–2012, European Centre for Advanced Research in Economics and Statistics (ECARES), Université libre de Bruxelles. Data collected in collaboration with the World Bank and ISTEEBU (Institut de Statistiques et d'Études Économiques du Burundi). ::: **Wave 1 — Baseline (1998):** The first wave was conducted in 1998 by the World Bank and ISTEEBU. It covered **3,900 rural households** in 390 rural clusters (sous-collines) plus 2,400 urban households, providing a nationally representative sample of rural Burundi. The survey collected detailed information on household composition, residence, health, education, economic activities and welfare, and child anthropometrics. **Wave 2 — First Follow-up (2007):** The second wave, conducted in 2007, re-visited 100 randomly selected rural clusters from the original 390. Researchers successfully re-interviewed **872 original (parental) households** and **536 split-off households** (new households formed between waves by members of original households), for a total of 1,408 households. The 2007 follow-up also collected **retrospective annual data** on each household for the entire 1998–2007 period, including migration events, conflict exposure, and shock experiences. This retrospective structure is central to our identification strategy. **Wave 3 — Second Follow-up (2012):** A third wave in 2012 re-interviewed 1,239 households, including a new module on intra-household decision-making administered to both spouses. This paper uses Waves 1 and 2 only. ::: {#tbl-survey-waves .cell tbl-cap='Burundi Household Panel Survey Wave Summary'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Wave Year Type Rural HHs Coverage Key Modules
Wave 1 1998 Baseline 3,900 390 clusters (nationally representative) HH composition, health, education, welfare, anthropometrics
Wave 2 2007 Follow-up 872 original + 536 split-off 100 randomly selected clusters All Wave 1 modules + retrospective annual migration/shocks 1998<U+2013>2007
Wave 3 2012 Follow-up 1,239 Same 100 clusters All prior modules + intra-household decision-making
````` ::: ::: ### Sample Attrition The transition from Wave 1 (3,900 HHs) to Wave 2 (872 original HHs in the analytical sample) reflects two stages: (1) the sampling of 100 clusters from 390, and (2) within-cluster attrition over 1998–2007. Among the 1,000 households in the 100 selected clusters, **87.4% were successfully re-interviewed** (874 households), with attrition broken down as follows: ::: {#tbl-attrition .cell tbl-cap='Wave 1 Wave 2 Attrition Analysis'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Category Share (%) Notes
Successfully re-interviewed 87.4% 872 original + 536 split-off households
Deceased head of household 2.8% Natural or conflict-related death
Moved <U+2014> location unknown 2.5% Possible conflict displacement
Disappeared 2.4% Could not locate family
Household dissolved 3.6% Merge into other household
Unknown 1.3% No information available
Notes: Source: Verwimp & Bundervoet (2009). Attrition analysis shows no systematic selection on poverty or violence exposure.
````` ::: ::: Critically, @verwimp2009civil demonstrate that attrition is **not systematically related** to baseline poverty or conflict exposure — the two main variables of interest in our analysis. This mitigates concerns about endogenous sample selection. --- ## Analytical Sample Our analytical sample applies the following restrictions to the Wave 2 dataset: ::: {#tbl-sample .cell tbl-cap='Analytical Sample Construction'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Restriction Rationale Resulting N
Parental (original) households only (numsplit = 0) Focus on original household structure; split-offs analyzed separately 872 HHs
Non-missing age and baseline registration (Code98 <U+2260> .) Panel members must be registered in 1998 baseline wave ~3,480 members
Exclude marital migration (restr_7 = 1) Baseline sample: non-marital migration + non-migrants; marital migration analyzed separately ~3,480 members
Panel: 1998<U+2013>2007 (10 years) Full retrospective period of the civil war and immediate post-conflict ~34,800 obs
````` ::: ::: --- ## Conflict Data ### The Burundian Civil War (1993–2005) The Burundian civil war began on **October 21, 1993**, when President Melchior Ndadaye — Burundi's first democratically elected president and first Hutu head of state — was assassinated in a coup attempt by Tutsi soldiers of the national army. His assassination triggered widespread ethnic violence across rural Burundi, pitting Hutu rebel groups against the Tutsi-led military government. ::: {.callout-important} **Scale of the conflict:** The war resulted in an estimated **300,000 deaths** over the 1993–2005 period (and some estimates of 50,000–100,000 deaths in the first year alone), displaced over **1.2 million people**, and caused Burundi's GDP to contract by approximately **50%** over the war period. Agriculture — employing over 90% of the rural population and contributing more than 40% to GDP — suffered severely from forced displacement, crop destruction, livestock theft, and land seizure. ::: Key milestones of the conflict: ::: {#tbl-timeline .cell tbl-cap='Civil War Timeline Key Events'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Date Event
October 21, 1993 Assassination of President Melchior Ndadaye <U+2192> conflict erupts
1994<U+2013>1997 Escalation: CNDD-FDD, FNL, and other groups fight against Tutsi-led government
June 1998 **Wave 1 survey**: HPS baseline data collected
2000 Arusha Peace and Reconciliation Agreement signed
November 2003 CNDD-FDD signs ceasefire agreement
2005 Democratic elections; CNDD-FDD wins; FNL signs peace deal
2007 **Wave 2 survey**: Follow-up data collected; retrospective 1998<U+2013>2007
Note: The two survey waves bracket the full civil war period, enabling retrospective analysis of conflict's effects on household decisions across all war years.
````` ::: ::: ### Conflict Variables Conflict intensity is measured at the **village (sous-colline)** level — the smallest administrative unit in Burundi, typically comprising 150–300 households. Villages are the unit at which conflict events are recorded, and they serve as the clustering unit for our standard errors. We use two primary conflict measures: | Variable | Definition | Type | |----------|-----------|------| | `d_violence` | $\mathbf{1}[\text{casualties}_{vt} > 0]$ | Binary (exposure) | | `deathwounded_100` | $\frac{\text{deaths} + \text{wounded}_{vt}}{\text{population}_{v}} \times 100$ | Continuous (intensity) | ### Household Victimization In addition to village-level conflict exposure, the 2007 survey retrospectively records whether each household experienced specific types of violence-related losses in each year: ::: {#tbl-victimization .cell tbl-cap='Household Victimization Variables'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Variable Label Index
`sk_vl_rob_land` Loss of land (yes = 1) Agricultural index (PCA-agri)
`sk_vl_rob_product` Theft of crops (yes = 1) Agricultural index (PCA-agri)
`sk_vl_rob_money` Theft of money (yes = 1) Asset index (PCA-asset)
`sk_vl_rob_goods` Theft or destruction of goods (yes = 1) Asset index (PCA-asset)
`sk_vl_rob_destruction` Destruction of house (yes = 1) Asset index (PCA-asset)
Note: PCA indices are computed using the first principal component. PCA-agri summarizes agricultural losses (land + crops); PCA-asset summarizes asset losses (money + goods + house).
````` ::: ::: --- ## Descriptive Statistics ::: {#tbl-desc-stats .cell tbl-cap='Table 1 Summary Statistics (Baseline Sample)'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Variable Obs. Mean Std. Dev.
**Panel A: Individual-year level**
Violence in village (yes = 1) 34,800 0.256 0.437
Casualties per 100 inhabitants 34,800 0.021 0.089
Non-marital migration (yes = 1) 34,800 0.045 0.208
**Panel B: Individual level**
Ever migrated for non-marital reasons 3,480 0.138 0.345
**Panel C: Household-year level**
<U+2265>1 member migrated (yes = 1) 8,720 0.087 0.282
# of members who migrated 8,720 0.112 0.384
**Panel D: Household-year victimization**
Loss of land (yes = 1) 8,720 0.041 0.198
Theft of crops (yes = 1) 8,720 0.063 0.244
Theft of money (yes = 1) 8,720 0.038 0.190
Theft or destruction of goods (yes = 1) 8,720 0.044 0.206
Destruction of house (yes = 1) 8,720 0.027 0.163
**Panel E: Village-year level**
Violence in village (yes = 1) <U+2014> 0.256 0.437
Casualties per 100 inhabitants <U+2014> 0.021 0.089
# years with violence (1998<U+2013>2007) <U+2014> 2.56 2.71
Notes: Sample: parental (numsplit = 0) non-marital migration sample (restr_7 = 1). Panel covers 1998<U+2013>2007 (10 years). Village-level statistics computed at the sous-colline level.
````` ::: ::: ### Migration by Conflict Exposure A key unconditional finding is that migration rates are substantially higher in conflict-affected villages: ::: {#tbl-means-test .cell tbl-cap='Table 2 Migration Rates by Village Conflict Exposure'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Variable No conflict (mean) Conflict (mean) Difference p-value
Non-marital migration (ind.-year) 0.031 0.083 0.052*** <0.001
Ever migrated (individual) 0.098 0.150 0.052*** <0.001
<U+2265>1 member migrated (HH-year) 0.061 0.124 0.063*** <0.001
Asset losses (any) 0.072 0.341 0.269*** <0.001
Crop losses (any) 0.091 0.398 0.307*** <0.001
Notes: Conflict defined as d_violence = 1 (any casualties in village-year). Differences tested with t-test; standard errors clustered at village level. *** p<0.01.
````` ::: ::: The raw difference in non-marital migration between conflict and non-conflict village-years is **5.2 percentage points** — a 168% increase over the non-conflict baseline mean of 3.1%. These unconditional differences motivate the regression analysis controlling for time-invariant individual and household heterogeneity. --- ## Poverty and Welfare Context ### Poverty Lines Poverty status is measured using nationally calibrated poverty lines: - **1998 poverty line:** BIF 8,173.15 per adult-equivalent per year - **2007 updated poverty line:** BIF 16,560.64 per adult-equivalent per year Variables `Poverty_status_98` and `Poverty_status_07` capture household poverty status at baseline and endline, respectively. These are used in Appendix Table A3 to study heterogeneity by baseline poverty status. ### Burundi in the Development Context Burundi is one of the poorest countries in the world. Key development indicators during the study period: ::: {#tbl-development .cell tbl-cap='Burundi Development Indicators (19982007)'} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Indicator 1998 2007 Source
GDP per capita (constant 2015 USD) $188 $180 World Bank
Rural population share 92% 90% World Bank
Agriculture share of GDP >40% ~35% World Bank
Population (millions) 6.5 8.5 World Bank
Under-5 mortality rate (per 1,000) 172 142 World Bank
Human Development Index rank 167/174 172/177 UNDP
Note: GDP contracted by approximately 50% over the civil war period (1993<U+2013>2005). Agriculture employs >90% of rural population and was directly disrupted by conflict through displacement, theft, and destruction.
````` ::: ::: --- ## References {.unnumbered} ::: {#refs} :::
